_meta.authoringNotes: Numbers cross-checked against Campbell SE (AJR 2001 PMID 11264071) on parameniscal cyst prevalence/sidedness, De Smet AA (Skeletal Radiol 2001) on MRI tear correlation, Lu KH (Arthroscopy 2003) on arthroscopic transmeniscal decompression, AAOS Clinical Practice Guideline on Meniscus Tears (2024), Reagan WD (AJSM 1989) on natural history of meniscal cysts, ISAKOS meniscus consensus (Kopf KSSTA 2020), and Lantz B (Clin Orthop 1990) for surgical outcome series.

h1Title: Medial Meniscus Cyst (Parameniscal Cyst): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A medial meniscus cyst is a fluid-filled outpouching that develops next to the inner-side meniscus of the knee, almost always from a horizontal cleavage tear that lets synovial fluid escape into the perimeniscal soft tissue. Medial parameniscal cysts are less common than their lateral counterparts: roughly 1% of medial meniscus tears develop a cyst, compared with 5-7% on the lateral side (Campbell, AJR 2001). Patients typically describe deep posteromedial joint-line pain that worsens with squatting, stair descent, and deep flexion, sometimes with a vague soft-tissue swelling that is harder to feel than a lateral cyst because the medial collateral ligament fibers cover it. MRI confirms the diagnosis in over 95% of cases by showing a T2-hyperintense fluid collection contiguous with a meniscal tear. Treatment is arthroscopic and addresses both the cyst and the underlying tear in a single operation.

keyStats.prevalence: Reported in roughly 1% of medial meniscus tears versus 5-7% of lateral tears (Campbell AJR 2001); overall meniscal cyst prevalence on knee MRI is approximately 4% with lateral predominance
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults aged 30-60; men slightly outnumber women; bilateral disease is rare
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence age 35-55, matching the age window for degenerative horizontal cleavage tears
keyStats.globalCases: No global registry; estimated incidence 1-2 per 100,000 person-years in symptomatic cohorts, with rising recognition from routine knee MRI use

definition: A medial meniscus cyst, also called a parameniscal cyst or cystic meniscus of the medial compartment (ICD-10: M23.0, cystic meniscus), is a localized collection of viscous synovial fluid that lies immediately adjacent to or within the medial meniscus and communicates with an underlying horizontal cleavage or complex degenerative tear. The mechanism is one-way valve extravasation: with each load cycle, synovial fluid is pumped from the joint through the meniscal tear into the perimeniscal recess, where the surrounding fibrous tissue contains and walls off the fluid. Over weeks to months a thin-walled pseudocyst develops, lined by compressed fibrous tissue rather than true epithelium. The medial cyst sits posteromedially in 70-80% of cases, behind the medial collateral ligament, and is far less palpable on physical examination than a lateral cyst because the MCL and pes anserinus tendons drape across it. Cyst contents are typically mucinous and clear, distinguishing them from hemorrhagic intra-articular collections. Untreated, cysts may slowly enlarge, fluctuate with activity, or rarely cause neural compression. Management is orthopedic, almost always arthroscopic, because cyst recurrence is over 50% if the meniscal tear that feeds it is not addressed at the same time.

typesClassification.description: The most common location, accounting for 70-80% of medial cysts. Sits behind the medial collateral ligament next to the posterior horn. Often presents with posteromedial joint-line pain and a vague non-palpable mass.
Less common, sits anterior to the MCL near the anterior horn. More likely to produce a palpable lump but smaller in size; pain is felt with deep flexion and direct pressure.

Fluid pocket contained within the substance of the meniscus without breaching the surface. Sometimes described as a meniscal mucoid degeneration cyst; symptoms milder, often discovered incidentally on MRI.

Variant in which the cyst tracks beyond the immediate parameniscal recess, sometimes burrowing along fascial planes into the proximal calf. Can mimic a Baker's cyst or deep vein thrombosis if it tracks posteriorly.

primarySymptoms: Deep, aching pain along the medial joint line that worsens with deep knee flexion, squatting, prolonged standing, and descending stairs, typically gradual in onset over weeks to months.
A vague soft-tissue fullness or swelling on the inner side of the knee, often less obvious than a lateral cyst because medial collateral ligament fibers and pes anserinus tendons cover it.
Posteromedial discomfort that is reproduced by palpation along the joint line behind the medial collateral ligament, sometimes radiating into the upper calf.
Mechanical symptoms of catching, clicking, or transient locking when the underlying horizontal cleavage tear traps tissue between the femur and tibia.
Stiffness after periods of inactivity and a sense of giving way on uneven ground, reflecting the meniscal tear's effect on rotational stability.
Pain that is worse at the end of the day or after sport, with relief from rest, ice, and NSAIDs over 24-48 hours.
Intermittent fluctuation of the swelling in size with activity, because cyst fluid is pumped in and out through the tear with each loading cycle.
Mild knee effusion noted by the patient as a tight feeling beneath the kneecap, usually small and not tense.
Reduced quadriceps strength and thigh circumference on the affected side after several weeks of guarded use, particularly in patients who limit deep flexion.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent medial joint-line pain that fails to improve after 4-6 weeks of activity modification and basic rehabilitation
A small fluctuant lump on the inner side of the knee that grows after activity and softens after rest
Catching or clicking sensation during squats or stair descent in a patient aged 35 and older
Discomfort with sleeping on the affected side because of pressure over the posteromedial joint line
emergencySigns: Calf swelling, redness, and tenderness with the medial knee pain — possible dissecting cyst mimicking or precipitating deep vein thrombosis and requiring urgent ultrasound
Fever with a hot, swollen, exquisitely tender knee — septic arthritis must be excluded by aspiration before any cyst intervention
Acute foot drop or numbness along the saphenous nerve distribution — rare neural compression from a large dissecting cyst
Sudden true mechanical locking of the knee that cannot be unlocked — suggests a displaced bucket-handle tear fragment in addition to the cyst and warrants urgent orthopedic referral

causes.cause: Horizontal cleavage tear of the medial meniscus
causes.description: The dominant cause. A degenerative split parallel to the tibial plateau creates a one-way conduit that pumps synovial fluid into the perimeniscal soft tissue. Over 90% of surgically treated medial cysts have an associated horizontal cleavage or complex tear (De Smet AA, Skeletal Radiol 2001).
Less common than horizontal tears but still capable of leaking synovial fluid outside the meniscus. Radial extension into the periphery widens the conduit and accelerates cyst growth.

Occupations requiring frequent squatting, kneeling, or stair climbing (flooring, plumbing, mining) and sports with cutting or deep flexion expose the meniscus to repeated shear and predispose to cleavage tears that later develop cysts.

After age 35-40 the meniscal collagen matrix accumulates mucoid material and microscopic clefts, lowering the threshold for cleavage tears under normal loads. This explains the peak incidence in the 35-55 age window.
Residual rim instability and altered biomechanics after meniscectomy can promote secondary tears and cyst formation in a previously operated meniscus, typically presenting 2-5 years after surgery.

riskFactors.description: Meniscal mucoid degeneration and horizontal cleavage tears accumulate with age, peaking in the fifth and sixth decades.
Men outnumber women roughly 1.5-2:1 in surgical series, largely reflecting occupational loading patterns rather than anatomic sex differences.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational squatting, kneeling, or heavy lifting
riskFactors.description: Jobs with cumulative deep-flexion loading (construction, flooring, agriculture) increase the risk of horizontal cleavage tears that seed cyst formation.
Soccer, basketball, tennis, and skiing impose repetitive shear on the medial meniscus, particularly the posterior horn. Risk persists into recreational midlife play.
Each unit of BMI increases medial compartment load and meniscal stress. Obesity is associated with higher prevalence of degenerative tears on cohort knee MRI (Englund NEJM 2008).

riskFactors.factor: Prior medial meniscus injury or surgery
riskFactors.description: A previously torn or partially resected meniscus is anatomically destabilized and develops new tears and cysts at higher rates than a virgin meniscus.
Varus malalignment shifts axial load onto the medial compartment, predisposing to medial meniscal degeneration and cleavage tears.

riskFactors.factor: Generalized joint hypermobility or connective tissue laxity
riskFactors.description: Mild hypermobility increases shear across meniscal tissue during pivot and is over-represented in younger patients with degenerative-pattern tears.

affectedDemographics: Most patients are working-age adults aged 35-60 with occupational or recreational deep-flexion exposure
Approximately 1% of medial meniscus tears develop a cyst versus 5-7% of lateral tears (Campbell AJR 2001) — medial cysts are the minority of all parameniscal cysts
Bilateral medial cysts are rare and should prompt evaluation for systemic crystalline arthropathy or generalized joint disease
Pediatric medial parameniscal cysts are exceptionally uncommon and typically associated with discoid meniscus or congenital anomaly rather than degenerative tear
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines history, focused examination, and MRI confirmation. Patients describe gradual posteromedial joint-line pain with mechanical symptoms; on examination, tenderness localizes to the medial joint line, especially behind the medial collateral ligament. McMurray's test for the medial meniscus reproduces pain in 60-70% of cases, but a palpable cystic mass is found in fewer than half of medial cysts because the MCL and pes anserinus drape over the cyst — a key clinical difference from lateral cysts, which are typically easy to feel as a tense bulge. MRI is the gold standard: a T2-hyperintense, well-defined fluid collection adjacent to the meniscus, contiguous with a horizontal cleavage or complex tear, is diagnostic with sensitivity and specificity over 95% (De Smet, Skeletal Radiol 2001). Grade III signal extending to the meniscal surface correlates with the underlying tear in essentially all symptomatic cases. High-resolution musculoskeletal ultrasound also detects medial cysts and helps differentiate them from Baker's cyst, pes anserine bursitis, MCL bursitis, and ganglion cysts of the proximal tibiofibular joint. Plain radiographs are usually normal but help assess concomitant medial compartment osteoarthritis, joint space narrowing, and varus alignment. Aspiration of the cyst is occasionally performed for diagnostic confirmation when imaging is equivocal: gelatinous mucoid fluid is characteristic. Septic arthritis, deep vein thrombosis (in the case of a dissecting cyst), and synovial sarcoma are the critical differentials that imaging and clinical context should exclude before elective intervention.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging. Confirms a T2-hyperintense fluid pocket adjacent to the medial meniscus, demonstrates the feeder tear (horizontal cleavage in over 90%), and stages medial compartment osteoarthritis and cartilage loss to plan surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan with the knee positioned in a dedicated coil; no contrast required for most cases. Patients with metal implants or claustrophobia may need alternative imaging.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bedside detection of a posteromedial fluid collection in patients who cannot undergo MRI. Helps differentiate medial cyst from Baker's cyst, MCL bursitis, pes anserine bursitis, and proximal tibiofibular ganglion. Also useful for guided aspiration when needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute painless scan with the knee flexed to 20-30 degrees; the sonographer applies gel and a probe along the medial joint line and posteromedial corner.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain weight-bearing radiographs (AP, lateral, skyline, Rosenberg)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates medial compartment joint space narrowing, varus alignment, and osteoarthritic changes that influence surgical planning and prognosis. Cysts themselves are radiolucent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Four standard knee X-rays taken weight-bearing in standing positions, lasting 5-10 minutes total. Required before any meniscal surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used selectively to confirm the diagnosis when imaging is equivocal or to provide short-term symptomatic relief. Recovery of clear gelatinous fluid is consistent with parameniscal cyst.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An outpatient procedure under local anesthetic taking 10-15 minutes. Mild soreness is expected for 24-48 hours; aspirated cysts recur in roughly 50-60% of cases within 6 months if the meniscal tear is not addressed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes deep vein thrombosis when a dissecting cyst tracks into the calf and produces leg swelling. Mandatory when calf symptoms accompany the medial knee findings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-30 minute painless leg scan performed in the vascular lab. No preparation is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for cases where imaging is equivocal or the surgeon needs intra-articular confirmation before definitive treatment. Allows direct visualization and probing of the meniscal tear and simultaneous cyst decompression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A day-case operation under general or spinal anesthesia, taking 30-45 minutes, with two or three small portal incisions and same-day discharge in most patients.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is staged according to symptom severity, tear pattern, age, and activity demands. Patients with mild symptoms and a small cyst on MRI can often start with 6-12 weeks of non-operative management: relative rest from deep-flexion activity, NSAIDs for pain, physiotherapy focused on quadriceps and hip strengthening, and weight reduction when relevant. Image-guided aspiration plus corticosteroid injection provides short-term relief in around 60% of patients but the cyst recurs in 50-60% within 6 months because the underlying meniscal tear continues to leak fluid — aspiration alone is therefore considered palliative rather than definitive. Surgical management is the standard when symptoms persist beyond 3 months, when the cyst is large or growing, or when mechanical symptoms (catching, locking) reflect an unstable tear. The modern approach is arthroscopic and addresses both pathologies in a single operation: the meniscal tear is repaired if it lies in the vascular red-red zone and the patient is a suitable candidate, or a partial meniscectomy is performed for irreparable tears with selective trimming back to a stable rim. Cyst decompression is performed through a transmeniscal arthroscopic approach — the surgeon directs the shaver through the tear into the cyst cavity to evacuate the fluid — which avoids the morbidity of an open posteromedial dissection through the MCL and pes anserinus. This is a key contrast with lateral cysts, where open decompression is occasionally still used. Reported pain relief after combined arthroscopic tear management plus cyst decompression is 80-90% at 2 years with recurrence rates under 10% in most series (Lu KH, Arthroscopy 2003; Reagan AJSM 1989). Long-term, patients with medial cysts face accelerated medial compartment osteoarthritis at 5-10 years follow-up because the medial meniscus carries 50-70% of medial compartment load, and any tissue removal raises contact stress on the cartilage below. Realistic counseling and ongoing strengthening and weight management remain important after surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for symptomatic pain and inflammation in the early weeks of conservative care or after surgery. Limit to 2-4 weeks at anti-inflammatory dose; reassess for renal, gastric, and cardiovascular contraindications in older patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate symptom relief in roughly 60% of patients with mild-to-moderate symptoms during the first 6 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Image-guided cyst aspiration with corticosteroid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Performed under ultrasound or fluoroscopic guidance using local anesthetic. Aspirates the cyst and instills a long-acting corticosteroid (triamcinolone 40 mg or methylprednisolone 40 mg) into the cyst cavity and adjacent joint to reduce inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Short-term symptom relief in 60-70% of patients but cyst recurrence in 50-60% within 6 months because the meniscal feeder tear is not treated.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physiotherapy program
medicalTreatments.description: Targets quadriceps strengthening (especially the vastus medialis), hip abductor and external rotator strengthening to offload the medial compartment, hamstring flexibility, and gait retraining. Equivalent or superior to surgery for degenerative tears without mechanical locking (ESCAPE trial, van de Graaf JAMA 2018).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 60-70% of patients with degenerative tears at 12 months; can postpone or avoid surgery in select cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification and load management
medicalTreatments.description: Temporary avoidance of squatting, deep flexion, kneeling, and high-impact pivot sports for 6-12 weeks, with gradual return guided by symptom response. Particularly effective when combined with weight loss in patients with BMI above 28.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Useful adjunct; meaningful symptom reduction in around 50% of patients when adhered to for at least 8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular hyaluronic acid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Sometimes considered in patients with coexistent medial compartment osteoarthritis and mild meniscal cyst symptoms. Reduces symptomatic inflammation rather than treating the cyst itself.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief for 3-6 months in approximately 50% of patients with knee osteoarthritis-overlap pattern.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic meniscal repair plus transmeniscal cyst decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Preferred procedure when the underlying tear is in the peripheral red-red or red-white vascular zone, the patient is under approximately 50 years old, and the tear pattern is amenable to suture. The cyst is decompressed through the tear with the arthroscopic shaver. Performed as a day-case under general or spinal anesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 85-90% with cyst recurrence under 10% when the repair heals; healing rates approximately 75-85% in carefully selected patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy plus transmeniscal cyst decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Used when the tear is irreparable (white-white avascular zone, complex degenerative pattern, older patient). The torn tissue is trimmed back to a stable rim and the cyst is decompressed through the same tract. Less tissue is removed than in older en-bloc resection techniques to preserve hoop tension.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 80-90% at 2 years; cyst recurrence 5-10%; long-term risk of accelerated medial compartment osteoarthritis at 5-10 years follow-up.

surgicalOptions.name: Open posteromedial decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Largely abandoned for primary medial cysts because of MCL and pes anserinus disruption. Occasionally used for very large recurrent cysts or dissecting cysts that cannot be reached arthroscopically. Recovery is slower and morbidity higher than the arthroscopic approach.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective in 80-85% of selected cases but rarely needed today.

surgicalOptions.name: Meniscal allograft transplantation (selected cases)
surgicalOptions.description: Considered in younger patients (typically under 50) who have undergone prior near-total medial meniscectomy and developed symptomatic medial compartment overload. Not a primary treatment for medial cysts themselves.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief in 70-80% at 5 years; survival of the allograft to 10 years approximately 70%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
alternativeTreatments.description: Sometimes offered in chronic medial joint pain with coexisting meniscal pathology. Evidence specifically for meniscal cyst is limited; benefit is more established in mild osteoarthritis and tendinopathy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and dry needling
alternativeTreatments.description: Adjunctive option for medial knee pain that does not address the underlying tear or cyst but may reduce symptomatic flares in selected patients with chronic pain features.

whySeeSpecialist: An orthopedic surgeon or sports medicine physician should evaluate any patient with persistent medial joint-line pain and an MRI-confirmed parameniscal cyst, particularly when mechanical symptoms (catching, locking) are present, the cyst is enlarging, or symptoms have failed 3 months of conservative care. Specialist input is essential for tear-pattern assessment, repair versus partial meniscectomy decision-making, and timing relative to coexisting cartilage or alignment problems. Primary care manages early conservative care effectively but should refer once surgery becomes a realistic consideration.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for fellowship-trained sports medicine or knee orthopedic surgeons with high arthroscopic volume, comfort with both meniscal repair and partial meniscectomy techniques, and routine use of transmeniscal cyst decompression rather than open posteromedial dissection. Ask about expected repair rates by tear type, recurrence rates in their own series, and the rehabilitation pathway they prefer. Access to a dedicated knee physiotherapist for postoperative care is at least as important as the surgical choice itself.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy body weight to reduce medial compartment load — every 5 kg of weight loss removes approximately 20 kg of force at the medial knee during stair descent
Strengthen quadriceps, hip abductors, and external rotators to offload the medial compartment, especially if your job or sport involves deep flexion
Use proper squatting and lifting mechanics with the knees tracking over the toes rather than collapsing inward
Wear well-cushioned footwear and limit prolonged kneeling at work, using knee pads where unavoidable
Address varus malalignment with appropriate orthotics or a medial unloader brace if recommended by your orthopedic team
lifestyleModifications: Substitute high-impact pivot sports with low-impact cross-training (swimming, cycling, elliptical) during symptomatic periods
Adopt a Mediterranean-style anti-inflammatory diet with adequate protein to support quadriceps strengthening
Sleep with a small pillow between the knees if posteromedial pain is provoked by side-lying
Schedule mid-day movement breaks if your work involves prolonged sitting, which stiffens the knee and worsens symptoms on standing
Stop smoking; nicotine impairs meniscal repair healing and overall musculoskeletal recovery

recommended: Lean protein at every meal (eggs, fish, chicken, legumes) to support quadriceps rebuilding during rehabilitation
Oily fish twice weekly for omega-3 anti-inflammatory effect
Leafy greens, berries, and colorful vegetables for vitamin C and polyphenols that aid collagen synthesis
Whole grains, nuts, and legumes for sustained energy and micronutrient density
Adequate water intake (at least 2 liters daily) to support synovial fluid and joint health
avoid: Excess ultra-processed food and sugar-sweetened beverages, which promote weight gain and worsen medial compartment load
Heavy alcohol intake, which impairs balance, sleep, and recovery from rehabilitation
Crash dieting that costs lean muscle mass; muscle protects the joint and should be preserved during weight loss
Smoking and chewing tobacco, which slow soft-tissue healing after meniscal repair

exerciseGuidelines: During the symptomatic period, replace deep squats, lunges, and high-impact pivot work with swimming, cycling on an upright bike with low resistance, and supervised quadriceps strengthening (straight-leg raises, terminal knee extensions, leg presses to 60 degrees of flexion). After arthroscopic tear management and cyst decompression, follow your surgeon's protocol: partial weight-bearing for 4-6 weeks if a repair was performed, immediate weight-bearing if only a partial meniscectomy was done. Plan a graded return to running at 6-8 weeks for meniscectomy patients and 4-5 months for repair patients, with pivot sport return at 6-9 months once strength and confidence are restored.
dailyManagement: Continue prescribed rehabilitation exercises daily for at least 12 weeks after symptom onset or surgery to consolidate strength gains
Use ice for 15-20 minutes after activity in the first 2 weeks to reduce swelling and posteromedial discomfort
Track symptom flares against activities (squatting, stair work, sport) in a simple log to identify avoidable triggers
Wear supportive footwear and consider a soft sleeve for mild compression during the working day if it improves comfort
Recheck weight every 4 weeks during rehabilitation; even 3-5 kg of loss measurably reduces medial joint load and symptom recurrence
prognosis: Short-term prognosis after arthroscopic tear management and cyst decompression is good: 80-90% of patients report meaningful pain relief at 2 years, with cyst recurrence rates under 10% when the feeder tear is addressed (Lu KH, Arthroscopy 2003; Reagan AJSM 1989). Return to work is typically 2-4 weeks for sedentary roles and 6-12 weeks for manual labor; return to recreational sport varies from 8 weeks after partial meniscectomy to 4-6 months after meniscal repair. Long-term prognosis is more cautious. The medial meniscus carries 50-70% of medial compartment load, and any tissue resection raises contact stress on the underlying cartilage. Cohort data show accelerated medial compartment osteoarthritis at 5-10 years after partial meniscectomy, with a doubling of total knee replacement risk in patients with degenerative tears (Englund Osteoarthritis Cartilage 2003). Meniscal repair, when feasible, partially preserves these biomechanics and produces better long-term outcomes than meniscectomy. Patients who maintain a healthy weight, sustained quadriceps strength, and avoid excessive deep-flexion loading retain the best joint function over the following decades.
recoveryTimeline: After arthroscopic partial meniscectomy with cyst decompression, immediate weight-bearing as tolerated is allowed; pain and swelling settle over 2-3 weeks and full daily function returns by 4-6 weeks. After meniscal repair plus cyst decompression, partial weight-bearing in a hinged brace is typical for 4-6 weeks, with progressive range of motion and strengthening over 3-4 months. Running typically resumes at 6-8 weeks for meniscectomy and 4-5 months for repair patients; pivot and cutting sports return at 4-6 and 6-9 months respectively. Cyst resolution on MRI is usually documented by 3 months if symptoms persist and re-imaging is needed.
complications: Cyst recurrence (10-15% at 2 years if the underlying meniscal tear is not adequately addressed)
Accelerated medial compartment osteoarthritis at 5-10 years, particularly after larger partial meniscectomy or in varus knees
Postoperative infection in approximately 0.5-1% of arthroscopic cases
Deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism, less than 1% with current prophylaxis but higher in older patients and those with prior VTE
Saphenous nerve injury during open posteromedial approaches, producing medial calf or foot numbness
Persistent stiffness or arthrofibrosis if rehabilitation is delayed or under-dosed after surgery

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Meniscus Tears Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education on meniscal tears, repair, and meniscectomy options.

supportResources.name: British Orthopaedic Association — Patient Information on Knee Conditions
supportResources.description: UK patient resources on knee disorders including meniscal injuries and arthroscopic surgery.

supportResources.name: Arthroscopy Association of North America (AANA)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resources on arthroscopic procedures, recovery, and rehabilitation expectations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lateral Meniscus Cyst (Parameniscal Cyst — lateral)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The lateral parameniscal cyst is much more common (5-7% of lateral tears vs roughly 1% on the medial side), almost always presents as an obvious palpable lateral joint-line lump, and is usually associated with a horizontal cleavage tear of the lateral meniscus. The medial cyst is harder to feel because MCL fibers cover it and tends to cause posteromedial joint-line pain instead of a visible bulge.

confusedWithConditions.name: Medial Meniscus Tear (Internal Derangement)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An isolated medial meniscus tear produces joint-line pain, swelling, and mechanical symptoms without a fluid-filled parameniscal collection on MRI. The medial meniscus cyst is a specific complication of a horizontal cleavage or complex tear in which synovial fluid extravasates into the perimeniscal soft tissue, producing a discrete T2-hyperintense cyst on MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meniscus Tear (general)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: General meniscal tears produce mechanical pain, clicking, and locking but no extra-meniscal cyst. A parameniscal cyst requires both a tear and a one-way valve leak of synovial fluid into surrounding tissue, visible as a discrete fluid pocket on MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Knee Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Knee osteoarthritis produces medial joint-line pain that worsens with use over years and is associated with joint-space narrowing and osteophytes on X-ray; a meniscal cyst is a focal fluid collection on MRI without those radiographic changes. The two often coexist in patients over 50.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ankle Sprain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ankle sprain presents as lateral or medial ankle pain after inversion injury and is unrelated to the knee; included here only because confused symptom mapping occasionally arises in patients with referred lower-limb pain.

relatedConditions.name: Meniscus Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: The underlying mechanism: nearly all medial parameniscal cysts arise from a horizontal cleavage or complex meniscal tear that lets synovial fluid escape into the perimeniscal soft tissue.

relatedConditions.name: Medial Meniscus Tear (Internal Derangement)
relatedConditions.relevance: The medial cyst is a specific complication of medial meniscus derangement, particularly degenerative horizontal cleavage tears in adults over 35.

relatedConditions.name: Knee Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Medial cysts coexist with medial compartment osteoarthritis in many older patients and accelerate cartilage loss after partial meniscectomy.

relatedConditions.name: ACL Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Concurrent ACL injury alters meniscal biomechanics and can predispose to traumatic medial tears that later develop a cyst.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inflammatory arthritis can produce synovial-fluid filled juxta-articular cysts that occasionally mimic parameniscal cysts on imaging and need to be differentiated.

faqs.question: What is a medial meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: A medial meniscus cyst is a fluid-filled pouch next to the inner-side cartilage of the knee. It forms when a horizontal cleavage tear in the meniscus lets joint fluid leak into the surrounding soft tissue and become walled off. Patients typically feel deep inner-knee pain that worsens with squatting and stairs.

faqs.question: How common is a medial meniscus cyst compared with a lateral one?
faqs.answer: Medial parameniscal cysts are much less common than lateral ones. About 1% of medial meniscus tears develop a cyst, compared with 5-7% on the lateral side. This is why a soft-tissue lump on the inside of the knee is harder to feel and easier to miss than the classic lateral meniscal cyst.

faqs.question: What causes a medial meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: The cause is a horizontal cleavage or complex tear of the medial meniscus, almost always degenerative in nature. Joint fluid is pumped through the tear with each step into the surrounding soft tissue, where it builds up over weeks to months as a thin-walled cyst. Repetitive squatting, kneeling, and pivot sports are typical risk factors.

faqs.question: Where does a medial meniscus cyst hurt?
faqs.answer: The pain is deep on the inside of the knee along the joint line, often felt behind the medial collateral ligament. It is usually worst with deep flexion, squatting, descending stairs, prolonged standing, and side-lying at night. Discomfort builds gradually and is relieved by rest, ice, and short courses of NSAIDs.

faqs.question: Why can I usually not feel a lump with a medial cyst?
faqs.answer: Unlike lateral cysts, which sit superficially under thin tissue, medial cysts lie behind the medial collateral ligament and pes anserinus tendons. These overlying structures spread and absorb the cyst, so a discrete lump is felt in fewer than half of cases. MRI is the reliable way to confirm the diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is a medial meniscus cyst diagnosed?
faqs.answer: MRI is the gold-standard test, showing a well-defined fluid pocket next to the meniscus contiguous with a horizontal cleavage tear. Accuracy exceeds 95% in experienced hands. Ultrasound is a useful bedside alternative, particularly when MRI is contraindicated, and helps rule out Baker's cyst, MCL bursitis, and pes anserine bursitis.

faqs.question: Can a medial meniscus cyst go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Small cysts in patients with mild symptoms can become quiescent with rest, weight loss, and quadriceps strengthening, but the underlying meniscal tear does not heal on its own in older adults. Most symptomatic cysts persist or recur within 6 months unless the feeder tear is treated, usually by arthroscopic surgery.

faqs.question: Is aspiration of the cyst enough?
faqs.answer: Aspiration removes fluid and provides short-term relief in around 60% of patients, but the cyst recurs in 50-60% within 6 months because the meniscal tear continues to leak fluid. Aspiration is a useful palliative or diagnostic step but is not considered a definitive treatment for symptomatic cysts.

faqs.question: What surgery is used for a medial meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: The standard operation is arthroscopic. The surgeon repairs the tear if it is in the peripheral vascular zone, or performs a partial meniscectomy if it cannot be repaired, then decompresses the cyst by directing the shaver through the tear into the cyst cavity. This avoids the morbidity of an open posteromedial dissection.

faqs.question: Why is open surgery rarely used for medial cysts?
faqs.answer: Open posteromedial decompression risks disrupting the medial collateral ligament and pes anserinus tendons and produces a slower recovery. Arthroscopic transmeniscal decompression treats both the tear and the cyst through small portals with much less soft-tissue morbidity. Open surgery is reserved for rare large recurrent or dissecting cysts.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after arthroscopy?
faqs.answer: After partial meniscectomy with cyst decompression, most patients are walking comfortably within 2 weeks and back to sedentary work in 2-4 weeks. Running typically returns at 6-8 weeks. After meniscal repair, weight-bearing is restricted for 4-6 weeks and pivot sports return at 6-9 months. A formal physiotherapy program is essential.

faqs.question: Will the cyst come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: When the underlying meniscal tear is adequately treated, cyst recurrence is under 10% at 2 years in most series. Recurrence is higher when only the cyst is decompressed without addressing the tear, or when a large radial component is left untreated. Adequate tear management is the single most important factor.

faqs.question: Does a medial meniscus cyst cause arthritis?
faqs.answer: A cyst by itself does not cause arthritis, but the underlying tear and any partial meniscectomy raise contact stress on the medial cartilage and accelerate osteoarthritis at 5-10 years. The medial meniscus carries 50-70% of medial compartment load, so preserving as much tissue as possible matters for long-term joint health.

faqs.question: How is a medial meniscus cyst different from a Baker's cyst?
faqs.answer: A Baker's cyst is a fluid collection at the back of the knee within the popliteal fossa, arising from the gastrocnemius-semimembranosus bursa and communicating with the joint through a valve. A medial meniscus cyst sits along the medial joint line and is contiguous with a meniscal tear on MRI. The two can coexist in patients with chronic effusion.

faqs.question: Can a medial meniscus cyst cause leg swelling?
faqs.answer: A small medial cyst causes only local fullness. Rarely, a large dissecting cyst tracks into the upper calf and produces leg swelling that can mimic deep vein thrombosis. Any unilateral calf swelling with medial knee pain should be evaluated urgently with Doppler ultrasound to rule out DVT before assuming a cyst origin.

faqs.question: Are women more affected than men?
faqs.answer: Men slightly outnumber women in surgical series for medial parameniscal cyst, roughly 1.5-2 to 1, largely reflecting occupational and sporting exposure rather than biological sex differences. Peak age is 35-55 in both sexes, matching the age window for degenerative horizontal cleavage tears.

faqs.question: What does the cyst fluid look like?
faqs.answer: When aspirated, cyst fluid is typically clear and gelatinous, much thicker than ordinary joint fluid because of its high hyaluronic acid content. Bloody or cloudy fluid suggests an alternative diagnosis such as hemorrhagic effusion, infection, or rarely a synovial tumor and warrants further evaluation.

faqs.question: Can I keep exercising with a medial meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: Low-impact activity such as swimming, cycling, and supervised strengthening is safe and helpful. Avoid deep squats, lunges, prolonged kneeling, and high-impact pivot sport until the cyst and tear are evaluated. Resuming aggressive deep-flexion loading typically aggravates symptoms and can enlarge the cyst over weeks.

faqs.question: Is this related to a sports injury?
faqs.answer: Most medial meniscus cysts arise from degenerative horizontal tears in adults over 35 rather than a single acute injury. However, repeated cutting and pivoting in soccer, basketball, tennis, and skiing accelerate meniscal degeneration and raise the chance of developing the tear that seeds the cyst.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist?
faqs.answer: See an orthopedic surgeon or sports medicine physician if medial knee pain persists beyond 6 weeks despite rest and rehabilitation, if mechanical symptoms such as catching or locking are present, if a lump appears on the inner knee, or if MRI confirms a parameniscal cyst. Early surgical opinion is also warranted in athletes with high activity demands.
